hero.tagline: Pennsylvania's Magisterial District Courts handle small claims up to $12,000 with a straightforward process.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Filing fee
at_a_glance.detail: Varies by claim amount and county

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in Court of Common Pleas

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If a claim exceeds the $12,000 cap, you cannot split it into multiple claims to fit within the limit. Instead, you must file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Pennsylvania allows you 4 years to file a claim for breach of contract, whether written or oral.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $10,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never completed the work.
claims.notes: You have 4 years from the date of breach to file. Bring the signed contract, payment records, and any correspondence.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend someone $5,000, and they failed to repay as promised.
claims.notes: Also a 4-year deadline. Gather evidence like emails, texts, or witness statements to prove the agreement.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a friend $3,000 with the understanding they'd repay in 6 months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: File within 4 years. Documentation of the loan terms and any repayment attempts will support your case.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $8,000 and missed the repayment deadline.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Present the original note and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $2,500 worth of consulting services, but the client hasn't paid the invoice.
claims.notes: File within 4 years. Keep copies of the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $1,200 on their store credit account.
claims.notes: 4-year statute of limitations. Maintain detailed account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $6,000 in back rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: File within 4 years. Lease agreements and payment records are crucial evidence.

claims.name: Receiving a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid with a $500 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: You can seek the check amount plus additional penalties. Provide the original check and bank notices.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer held liable for default
claims.example: You co-signed a $7,000 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted.
claims.notes: 4-year limit. Present the loan agreement and records of default.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Pennsylvania's Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Law allows consumers to seek remedies for deceptive practices.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,000 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: File within 6 years. Keep purchase receipts and any communication with the seller.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $100, whichever is greater; treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $500 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: 6-year statute. Document the service agreement and any complaints made.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $100, whichever is greater; treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory auto repair
claims.example: Paid $2,000 for car repairs that were not completed properly, causing further damage.
claims.notes: File within 6 years. Maintain repair invoices and expert evaluations.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $100, whichever is greater; treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: Home contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: A contractor was paid $8,000 to renovate your bathroom but left the job unfinished.
claims.notes: 6-year limit. Keep the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $100, whichever is greater; treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods/services
claims.example: Paid $1,500 for catering services, but the vendor never showed up.
claims.notes: File within 6 years. Provide contracts and proof of payment.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $100, whichever is greater; treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: Cancelled a $50/month gym membership, but charges continued for 3 months.
claims.notes: 6-year statute. Keep cancellation confirmations and bank statements.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $100, whichever is greater; treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: Bought a used car for $9,000 that broke down within a week due to undisclosed issues.
claims.notes: File within 6 years. Retain the sales agreement and any repair records.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $100, whichever is greater; treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: Purchased a $200 product advertised as 'new,' but it was refurbished.
claims.notes: 6-year limit. Save advertisements and purchase receipts.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $100, whichever is greater; treble damages for willful violations.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Claims for property damage in Pennsylvania generally have a 2-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver caused $4,000 in damage to your car and refuses to pay.
claims.notes: File within 2 years. Obtain police reports and repair estimates.

claims.name: Neighbor's actions damaged your property
claims.example: Neighbor's tree fell on your fence, causing $1,500 in damage.
claims.notes: 2-year statute. Take photos and get repair quotes.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: Tenant left $3,000 worth of damage upon moving out.
claims.notes: File within 2 years. Document damages with photos and repair bills.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone's pet
claims.example: Neighbor's dog chewed your $800 outdoor furniture.
claims.notes: 2-year limit. Gather evidence like photos and witness statements.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: Dry cleaner ruined your $500 suit and refuses to compensate.
claims.notes: File within 2 years. Keep receipts and damaged items as evidence.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Pennsylvania law provides specific protections and remedies for both landlords and tenants in financial disputes.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Landlord withheld your $1,200 security deposit without justification.
claims.notes: Landlord must return deposit within 30 days. File within 2 years if not returned.

claims.name: No itemized list for deposit deductions
claims.example: Landlord deducted $500 from your deposit without providing an itemized list.
claims.notes: Landlord must provide an itemized list within 30 days. File within 2 years if not provided.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Persistent mold made your $900/month apartment unlivable, and landlord refused to address it.
claims.notes: File within 2 years. Document conditions and communication with landlord.

claims.name: Landlord retained personal property after move-out
claims.example: Landlord kept your $2,000 worth of furniture after you vacated.
claims.notes: File within 2 years. Provide proof of ownership and communication records.

claims.name: Illegal eviction without proper notice
claims.example: Landlord changed locks without notice, preventing access to your $1,500/month apartment.
claims.notes: File within 2 years. Keep records of lease and eviction notice (or lack thereof).

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: Landlord didn't refund your $1,000 prepaid rent after you legally terminated the lease.
claims.notes: File within 2 years. Provide lease agreement and termination notice.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent owed
claims.example: Tenant owes $4,500 in unpaid rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: File within 4 years. Present lease agreement and payment records.

claims.name: Tenant deducted repair costs from rent
claims.example: Tenant deducted $300 for repairs from rent without landlord's consent.
claims.notes: File within 2 years. Provide lease terms and repair receipts.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Pennsylvania's Wage Payment and Collection Law allows employees to recover unpaid wages and penalties.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but hasn't paid.
claims.notes: File within 3 years. Keep pay stubs and records of hours worked.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After termination, employer didn't provide your $1,500 final paycheck.
claims.notes: File within 3 years. Document termination and owed wages.

claims.name: Unpaid sales commissions
claims.example: Employer owes $3,000 in earned commissions but refuses to pay.
claims.notes: File within 3 years. Provide commission agreements and sales records.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: Worked 10 hours of overtime but received no additional pay.
claims.notes: File within 3 years. Maintain timesheets and pay records.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Personal injury claims in Pennsylvania have a 2-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: Slipped on a wet floor in a store, resulting in a $1,000 medical bill.
claims.notes: File within 2 years. Obtain medical records and incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: Loaned a friend your $500 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 2-year limit. Provide proof of ownership and loan agreement.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: Neighbor took your $800 bicycle without permission and won't return it.
claims.notes: File within 2 years. Gather evidence of ownership and theft.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Claims related to vehicle repairs and towing in Pennsylvania have specific legal considerations.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair charges
claims.example: Mechanic charged $1,200 for unauthorized repairs on your car.
claims.notes: File within 6 years. Keep repair orders and invoices.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $100, whichever is greater; treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: Vehicle wrongfully towed
claims.example: Car was towed from a legal parking spot, costing $300 in fees.
claims.notes: 6-year statute. Obtain towing records and photos of parking area.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $100, whichever is greater; treble damages for willful violations.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction proceedings are handled separately and are not within the jurisdiction of small claims court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in Magisterial District Court under landlord-tenant proceedings.

exclusions.explanation: Disputes involving ownership of real property exceed the jurisdiction of small claims court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in the Court of Common Pleas.

exclusions.explanation: Claims for libel or slander are complex and not suitable for small claims court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Consult an attorney for appropriate filing.

exclusions.explanation: Malpractice claims require expert testimony and are beyond small claims jurisdiction.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in the Court of Common Pleas.

exclusions.explanation: Divorce, child custody, and support issues are handled by family courts.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in the Family Division of the Court of Common Pleas.

exclusions.explanation: Estate and will disputes are under the jurisdiction of the Orphans' Court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in the Orphans' Court Division of the Court of Common Pleas.

exclusions.explanation: Federal entities are not subject to state small claims court jurisdiction.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in the appropriate federal court.

exclusions.explanation: Class action lawsuits are complex and exceed small claims court procedures.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in the Court of Common Pleas.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims court cannot order someone to do or stop doing something.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in the Court of Common Pleas.

exclusions.explanation: These claims are handled by the state's workers' compensation system.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File with the Pennsylvania Department of Labor & Industry.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.
Actual damages or $100, whichever is greater; treble damages for willful violations.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable in cases involving bad checks, consumer protection violations, and unpaid wages.

filing_options: Any county where ONE defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing availability varies by county; check with the local Magisterial District Court.

service_fees.notes: Varies by county; required for most cases.
Available for certain cases; confirm with court.

forms.name: Petition to Proceed In Forma Pauperis

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: Waiver covers filing fees; service fees may still apply.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: Waived fees are not added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fee and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge
Date fraud was discovered or should have been discovered
Date deposit was due to be returned

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered or should have been discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy stays.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention. For bad check claims, a 30-day notice is required.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: Before filing a claim for a bad check, you must send a written demand for payment, allowing the issuer 30 days to respond.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims under Pennsylvania law.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide a forwarding address for the return of security deposits. Tenants should document repair requests and notices.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing notice required under Pennsylvania's Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Law.

forms.name: Civil Complaint
forms.description: Initiates a civil action in Magisterial District Court.

forms.name: Notice of Judgment
forms.description: Notifies parties of the court's judgment.

forms.name: Petition to Proceed In Forma Pauperis
forms.description: Requests waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Initiates an appeal to the Court of Common Pleas.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally delivers the complaint to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail
methods.description: Complaint is sent via certified mail with return receipt requested.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A private individual authorized to serve legal documents delivers the complaint.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Pennsylvania's long-arm statute and the rules of the state where the defendant resides.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, the court may allow service by publication in a newspaper of general circulation.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, you may request the court to permit alternative methods of service.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendant is not required to file a written answer but must appear at the hearing. Failure to appear may result in a default judgment.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must present evidence to support the claim and amount of damages.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal to the Court of Common Pleas within 30 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds the jurisdictional limit, the case may be transferred to the Court of Common Pleas.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys but are not required to do so.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter from the court at least 5 days before the hearing.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within 5 days.

appeals.type_notes: The appeal is a new trial in the Court of Common Pleas.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed within 30 days to the Court of Common Pleas.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues at 6% per annum on unpaid judgments.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of execution with the court, serve the employer, and the employer will withhold a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution, serve the bank, and the bank will freeze and remit funds from the debtor's account.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File the judgment with the county recorder to place a lien on the debtor's property, which must be satisfied upon sale or refinancing.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Authorize the sheriff to seize and sell the debtor's personal property.
methods.description: File a writ of execution, and the sheriff will seize and auction the debtor's non-exempt personal property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt personal property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a revival of judgment before the 20-year period expires to extend the judgment's enforceability.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge certain judgments; consult an attorney for specifics.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among judgment creditors is generally determined by the date of lien attachment.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can appear without an attorney in Magisterial District Court.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a fictitious name must register with the state and may need to provide proof in court.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may authorize an employee to represent them in court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face challenges in enforcing contracts; consult legal counsel.

county_variations.detail: Philadelphia has a separate Municipal Court handling small claims up to $12,000.
Allegheny County offers e-filing for certain cases; check with the local court.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: Suing the wrong party or using incorrect names can result in dismissal or unenforceable judgments.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the exact legal name and address of the defendant before filing.

pitfalls.title: Missing statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the deadline results in automatic dismissal of the case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Confirm the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type before filing.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to serve the defendant correctly can delay the case or lead to dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow the court's rules for service of process precisely.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of evidence can result in losing the case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements before the hearing.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: Defendants may file counterclaims, potentially increasing your liability.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be prepared to defend against possible counterclaims by reviewing your case thoroughly.

pitfalls.title: Overlooking post-judgment procedures
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment doesn't guarantee payment; you must take steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with collection methods and follow through promptly.

recent_changes.title: Increase in filing fees
recent_changes.description: Filing fees for small claims cases increased by $10 across all tiers.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in Magisterial District Court. Many individuals represent themselves in small claims cases.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Small claims cases typically resolve within 30 to 90 days, depending on the court's schedule and complexity of the case.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in Pennsylvania's small claims court is $12,000.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal the judgment to the Court of Common Pleas within 30 days.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Pennsylvania is valid for 20 years and can be renewed before expiration.

state_specific_notes: Pennsylvania allows treble damages for willful violations under its consumer protection law.
Security deposits must be returned within 30 days, or landlords face double damages.
Wage garnishment is limited to 25% of disposable earnings.
Post-judgment interest accrues at 6% per annum.
Philadelphia has a separate Municipal Court for small claims up to $12,000.

interesting_findings: Pennsylvania's small claims court allows for statutory penalties in bad check cases, potentially doubling the amount owed.
The state provides a standardized form for filing in forma pauperis petitions, aiding those unable to afford filing fees.
Magisterial District Courts offer a relatively quick resolution for small claims, often within 90 days.
